1:
"The first day of the week cometh Mary Magdalene early, when it was yet dark, unto the sepulchre, and seeth the stone taken away." The witnesses read the opening of the resurrection narrative — and the care with which John establishes its central fact.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the time and the harmony. Robertson: the idiom "on the first day of the week" (tē mia tōn sabbatōn), shared with Mark 16:2 and Luke 24:1. Barnes refers the fuller account to Matthew 28:1. Ellicott notes John relates "only that announcement of which he had special personal knowledge," each Gospel separating the visit from the angelic appearance. Clarke summarizes the chapter's flow — the empty tomb, the race of Peter and John, the vision of angels, and Christ's appearing to Mary.

The Eastern father reads Mary's devotion. Chrysostom: "the Sabbath being now over, during which it was unlawful to be there, Mary Magdalene could rest no longer, but came very early to seek consolation at the grave."

The Reformed and Puritan voices weigh the importance of the proof. Calvin: "as the resurrection of Christ is the most important article of our faith, and without it the hope of eternal life is extinguished, the Evangelists are the more careful to prove it," John "collecting many proofs." Henry: "there was no one thing of which the apostles were more concerned to produce substantial proof than the resurrection of their Master" (Acts 1:3). So the witnesses agree: John opens with sober, datable fact — a woman, a dawn, a stone rolled away — beginning the careful accumulation of evidence on which the church's whole hope rests (v.31).

The witnesses thus open the resurrection account on sober ground: a named woman, a dated dawn, a stone removed. John, who will end by declaring his whole purpose (v.31), begins by laying the first stone of evidence, knowing that on this fact alone the church's hope of eternal life stands or falls (1 Cor 15:14).

Chrysostom marks her love: the Sabbath over, she 'could rest no longer,' but came while it was yet dark to seek consolation at the grave.
2:
"Then she runneth, and cometh to Simon Peter, and to the other disciple, whom Jesus loved, and saith unto them, They have taken away the Lord." The witnesses read Mary's alarmed report — she assumes the body has been removed, not raised.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scene and the harmony. Robertson: the vivid dramatic present "runneth"; John "deals only with Mary Magdalene," who "left the tomb at once before the rest and without seeing the angels" of the Synoptics (Mark 16:2-8; Matthew 28:5-8). Ellicott: "St. John relates only that announcement of which he had special personal knowledge"; "the other disciple" is John himself (John 13:23; John 19:26). Clarke: she told not Peter and John only but the other apostles too (Matthew 28:8), yet only these two ran to the tomb.

Gill notes she could say "taken away out of it" only because she had looked in and seen the body gone. Her words breathe distress, not yet faith: "we know not where they have laid him."

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem reads the devotion behind the haste: "the bridegroom and suitor of souls was sought by those noble and brave women." So the witnesses agree: Mary's report is true in its observation — the tomb is empty — but mistaken in its inference; she supposes theft where there has been resurrection. Her running to the disciples sets in motion the eyewitness investigation that will turn supposed loss into proof of the rising (v.3).

The witnesses thus mark the gap between true observation and false inference: the tomb is empty, but Mary supposes theft, not rising. Her distressed running to the disciples sets in motion the eyewitness investigation that will turn supposed loss into the first proof of the resurrection (v.8).

Her words breathe distress, not faith — 'we know not where they have laid him' — the cry of love bereaved, not yet of love expectant (v.13).

Her running sets the eyewitness inquiry in motion, and supposed loss will become the first proof of the rising (v.8).
3:
"Peter therefore went forth, and that other disciple, and came to the sepulchre." A brief verse, and the witnesses read the prompt response of the two disciples to Mary's report.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the detail. Robertson: the imperfect "they were going," picturing the scene, the two having "started instantly." Ellicott: the details of this visit (verses 3–10) are "peculiar to this Gospel"; Luke mentions Peter only (Luke 24:12), but here "we have the whole scene pictured with all the vividness and exactness of one who stated what he himself saw and took part in."

The Reformed voice reads the faith behind the zeal. Calvin: "there being so little faith, or rather almost no faith, both in the disciples and in the women, it is astonishing that they had so great zeal" — yet "some seed of faith remained in their hearts, but quenched for a time." Gill: Peter "went forth... upon hearing the account Mary gave."

So the witnesses agree: the verse records the disciples' instant, eager movement toward the tomb — a zeal that, Calvin observes, outran their faith, since they did not yet expect a risen Lord but only to investigate a disturbing report. The eyewitness vividness of the account (v.4) is itself a mark of its truthfulness, John recording what he and Peter saw and did. Their haste, springing from a love stronger than their understanding, will be rewarded with the first sight of the grave-clothes that point beyond theft to resurrection.

The witnesses thus read the disciples' instant movement as a zeal that, Calvin observes, outran their faith — they expected no risen Lord but only to test a troubling report. The eyewitness vividness of the scene that follows is itself a quiet token of its truthfulness (v.5).

The details of this visit, peculiar to John, bear the vividness of one who saw and took part in them (Lk 24:12).

Their love, Calvin notes, outran a faith that did not yet expect a risen Lord but only to test a troubling report.

Their haste, springing from a love stronger than their understanding, is rewarded with the first sight of the grave-clothes (v.5).
4:
"So they ran both together: and the other disciple did outrun Peter, and came first to the sepulchre." The witnesses read the small, vivid detail — and resist over-allegorizing it.

The grammatical-historical readers keep it simple. Robertson: "it was a race in eagerness to reach the tomb of Jesus"; "outran Peter" (proedramen). Ellicott: the outrunning "is simply the result of the greater activity of John, who was probably younger"; "the thought that love outran doubt or fear... is not in harmony with the context, for Peter therefore went forth" as eagerly. Gill: "not content to walk, they ran, being eagerly desirous to know the truth."

The witnesses thus agree on a deliberately plain reading: the younger disciple simply ran faster. The detail is not a parable of love versus zeal but the natural recollection of one who was there — the kind of incidental particular that marks an eyewitness account rather than a later invention.

So the verse stands, across the schools, as a quiet token of the narrative's authenticity: John remembers that he reached the tomb first, and says so without making it a symbol. The race itself testifies to the disciples' love and urgency, and the arrival sets the stage for what John sees on stooping in — the grave-clothes lying undisturbed, the first physical evidence that no thief had been at work (v.5). The honesty of such small details lends weight to the great fact they serve.

The witnesses thus keep the detail plain: the younger disciple simply ran faster, and John records it without making it a symbol. Such incidental honesty is the mark of a true memory, not a later invention, and it serves the great fact it adorns (v.8).

The race itself testifies to the disciples' love and urgency, even where their understanding still lagged behind.

John records that he reached the tomb first without making it a symbol — the honest particular of a true memory.

Such incidental honesty lends weight to the great fact it serves, the resurrection it was written to attest and the faith it was meant to confirm.
5:
"And he stooping down, and looking in, saw the linen clothes lying; yet went he not in." The witnesses read John's first glimpse into the tomb — the undisturbed grave-clothes that already tell against theft.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the picture. Robertson: parakypsas, "to stoop and look," or simply "to peep in"; the present "seeth the linen clothes lying." Ellicott: "the tense still describes the scene as it actually occurred." Lightfoot details the burial-cave, the body laid "four cubits lower than the floor."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the inference: John "went not in" because "he was fully satisfied that the body was not there" — and pointedly notes he did not seize the linen as a relic, "because he had too much religion and too much sense; the time of superstition... was not yet arrived."

The Reformed voice reads the evidential force. Calvin: the linen clothes "might be regarded as the spoils, intended to lead to the belief of Christ's resurrection; for it was not probable that his body had been stripped naked, in order that it might be removed" — "this would not have been done by a friend, nor even by an enemy." So the witnesses agree: the orderly grave-clothes are the first silent witness to the resurrection — a body stolen would have been carried off wrapped, not stripped and the wrappings left behind; John sees this and pauses at the threshold, leaving Peter to enter first and examine more closely (v.6).

The witnesses thus hear the grave-clothes preach: a stolen body would have been carried off wrapped, not stripped and the wrappings left. John pauses at the threshold, the first silent evidence already before his eyes, leaving Peter to enter and examine more closely (v.6).

Clarke notes John did not seize the linen as a relic, 'the time of superstition not yet arrived' — a sober restraint the witnesses commend.

A body removed by friend or foe, Calvin observes, would not be stripped and the wrappings left lying in order.

The orderly linen is the first silent witness, and John leaves the closer examination to the bolder Peter (v.6).
6:
"Then cometh Simon Peter following him, and went into the sepulchre, and seeth the linen clothes lie." The witnesses read the impulsive Peter entering where John had paused, to examine closely.

The grammatical-historical readers mark the verbs. Robertson: "Peter impulsively went on in and beholds" (theōrei, "a vivid term... of careful notice," not the mere glance blepō John gave from outside). Clarke on the same word: "to look steadily at anything, so as to discover what it is." Ellicott: it "expresses the close observation of the linen clothes by St. Peter, while St. John did but see them from without."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley anticipates the next verse: "Peter seeth the linen clothes lie, and the napkin folded up — the angels who ministered to him when he rose undoubtedly folded up the napkin and linen clothes."

(A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here concerns the use of Scripture and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: the difference in the verbs is no accident but the recollection of one who was there — John glanced in, Peter went in and studied. The two disciples together establish, by careful observation, the orderly state of the tomb: the grave-clothes undisturbed, the body gone. Peter's bolder entry draws John in after him, and the closer they look, the clearer it becomes that this was no robbery but a rising (v.7).

The witnesses thus note the eyewitness distinction of verbs — John glanced from without, Peter went in and studied. Together they establish by careful observation the orderly state of the tomb, and the closer they look the plainer it grows that this was no robbery but a rising (v.7).

The bolder Peter draws John in after him, so that the two together examine and confirm the orderly emptiness of the tomb.

The careful eye notices what the casual glance misses, and what Peter beholds confirms that no thief had been at work.

The two disciples together establish, by careful sight, that the body was gone and the wrappings undisturbed, no robbery but a rising.
7:
"And the napkin, that was about his head, not lying with the linen clothes, but wrapped together in a place by itself." The witnesses read this minute detail as decisive evidence against the theft theory.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the picture. Robertson: soudarion, the head-napkin (John 11:44), "rolled up" (entetyligmenon), "arranged in an orderly fashion. There was no haste." Ellicott: "in this description... the minute knowledge and remembrance of an eye-witness" is plain. Clarke draws the inference: "if the body had been stolen, those who took it would not have stopped to strip the clothes from it, and to wrap them up, and lay them by in separate places" — "the providence of God ordered these very little matters" to refute the lie that the disciples stole him (Matthew 28:13).

The Reformed voice turns it against relic-mongering. Calvin: the napkin "wrapped by itself" "refutes the falsehood of the Papists, who pretend that the whole body was sewed up in one linen garment, which they hold out to the wretched populace, calling it the holy winding-sheet."

So the witnesses agree: the folded napkin, set apart from the linen, is the orderly signature of one who rose unhurried, not of robbers working in haste. The very smallness of the detail is its strength — no inventor would think to record it, but an eyewitness would, and its quiet testimony overturns the official story of a stolen body. The grave-clothes, calmly disposed, preach the resurrection before any angel speaks (v.8).

The witnesses thus make the folded napkin the orderly signature of one who rose unhurried, not of robbers working in haste. The very smallness of the detail is its strength: no inventor would record it, but an eyewitness would, and its quiet testimony overturns the official tale of a stolen body (Mt 28:13).

Calvin turns the separate napkin against the relic of a single 'holy winding-sheet,' the order of the grave preaching an unhurried rising.

The very smallness of the detail is its strength, for no inventor would record it, but an eyewitness would (Mt 28:13).
8:
"Then went in also that other disciple, which came first to the sepulchre, and he saw, and believed." The witnesses read John's own confession of faith — and debate just what he then believed.

The grammatical-historical readers and Reformed voice incline to incipient resurrection-faith. Robertson: "Peter saw more after he entered than John did in his first glance, but John saw into the meaning of it all better than Peter" — Peter had "more sight," John more insight. Calvin rejects the thin reading that John merely believed "what he had heard Mary say," that the body was carried away: "there is no passage in which the word bears this meaning" when used "simply and without any addition."

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices read it more modestly. Clarke: "he saw that the body was not there, and believed that it had been taken away, as Mary had said; but he did not believe that he was risen from the dead — see what follows" (v.9). Wesley likewise: "he believed that they had taken it away as Mary said."

So the witnesses divide: did John, seeing the orderly grave-clothes, begin to believe Christ was risen (Robertson, Calvin, Ellicott), or only that the body was gone (Clarke, Wesley)? The next verse — "as yet they knew not the Scripture" — is read by the latter as proof that full resurrection-faith had not yet dawned, and by the former as the very thing the sight of the tomb began to teach. All agree John's faith was real but not yet grounded in understanding; the empty tomb had spoken, but the Scripture's witness was not yet grasped (v.9).

The witnesses thus divide over what John believed — incipient resurrection-faith (Robertson, Calvin) or only that the body was gone (Clarke, Wesley) — but agree his faith was real and not yet grounded in understanding. The empty tomb had spoken; the Scripture's witness was not yet grasped (v.9).

All agree the empty tomb had spoken, though the full meaning of the Scripture that foretold the rising was not yet grasped (v.9).
9:
"For as yet they knew not the scripture, that he must rise again from the dead." The witnesses read the disciples' failure to have grasped the Old Testament's witness to the resurrection.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the Scripture. Robertson: "probably Psalm 16:10"; "Jesus had repeatedly foretold his resurrection, but that was all forgotten in the great sorrow" — only the chief priests recalled his words (Matthew 27:62-63); the divine "must" (Luke 24:26). Barnes lists the predictions — Psalm 16:9-10 (Acts 2:25-32), Psalm 110:1 (Acts 2:34-35). Clarke gives the Hebrew of Psalm 16:10 and calls their ignorance "a reproach."

The Reformed voice reads the dawning. Calvin: "they had often heard from the mouth of Christ what they now saw with their eyes," but understanding "flowed from their hearts"; warned by the strange sight, "they begin to think of Christ as having something Divine, though still far from a clear knowledge." Ellicott: from that moment "they recognised in the fact of the absent body... the truth that he must rise again."

So the witnesses agree: the disciples' resurrection-faith was, at this point, the fruit of sight more than of Scripture — they had not yet understood the prophecies (chiefly Psalm 16:10) that foretold the rising, nor recalled Christ's own words. The verse is at once a candid confession of their dullness and a testimony to the resurrection's reality: they were not predisposed to expect it, and so were no credulous witnesses but men convinced against their settled incomprehension (v.8).

The witnesses thus make the disciples' ignorance a testimony to the resurrection's reality: they were not predisposed to expect it, having forgotten both the Scripture (chiefly Ps 16:10) and Christ's own words, and so were no credulous witnesses but men convinced against their settled incomprehension (v.8).

Calvin reads a faith dawning from sight before Scripture — warned by the strange spectacle, they begin to sense something Divine, though far from clear knowledge.

Their want of expectation makes them no credulous witnesses but men convinced against their own settled incomprehension.
10:
"Then the disciples went away again unto their own home." A brief verse, and the witnesses read the two disciples returning, their faith still mingled with uncertainty — and the stage cleared for Mary's encounter.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the sense. Robertson: "to themselves," i.e. their own home (the French chez eux); John "had taken the mother of Jesus to his home" (John 19:27) and now "hurried home to tell her." Ellicott: "to their lodgings in Jerusalem," their object accomplished — "one, at least, had realised" that the Lord was not to be looked for in the empty grave.

The Latin and Eastern fathers contrast the departing men with the lingering Mary. Gregory: "Mary Magdalene... whose soul burned with love, did not retire from the sepulchre when the others did." Chrysostom: "full of feeling somehow is the female sex, and more inclined to pity," so Mary wept on "while Peter was in no way so affected."

The Reformed voice reads the disciples' lingering doubt. Calvin: "it is possible that their minds were still in a state of doubt... their faith was not strong, but was only some confused remembrance of the miracle, and resembled a trance, until it was more fully confirmed." So the witnesses agree: Peter and John, having seen, return home with a faith real but unsettled, while Mary's deeper love keeps her at the tomb — and it is to her persevering devotion, not to the apostles' investigation, that the risen Lord will first show himself (v.11).

The witnesses thus contrast the departing men with the lingering Mary: Peter and John return home with a faith real but unsettled, while her deeper love keeps her at the tomb. It is to that persevering devotion, not to the apostles' investigation, that the risen Lord will first show himself (v.11).

Gregory and Chrysostom contrast the departing men with the lingering woman, whose burning love would not leave the place of her loss (v.11).

It is to that persevering love, not to the apostles' investigation, that the risen Lord will first appear (v.11).
11:
"But Mary stood without at the sepulchre weeping: and as she wept, she stooped down, and looked into the sepulchre." The witnesses read the constancy of Mary's grief-stricken love, which keeps her at the tomb when the others have gone.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scene. Robertson: the past perfect "was standing," "a pathetic and common picture of a woman weeping by the tomb"; the imperfect "as she was weeping." Ellicott: she had gone back to tell the disciples (vv.1–2), then followed them, and now that they have returned home she remains, alone. Clarke: "she remained some time after Peter and John had returned."

The Puritan Henry marks "the constancy and fervency of Mary Magdalene's affection" — Mark records that Christ "appeared first to Mary Magdalene" (Mark 16:9).

The Reformed voice reads the providence and the company. Calvin: the Evangelist begins "to describe the manner in which Christ appeared"; though he mentions one woman, "it is probable that the other women were also along with her." So the witnesses agree: Mary's persevering love — weeping, stooping, looking yet again into the tomb she had already found empty — is the disposition to which the risen Lord draws near. Her devotion outlasts the apostles' investigation, and her very inability to leave the place of her loss becomes the occasion of the first resurrection appearance, beginning with the vision of angels (v.12).

The witnesses thus read Mary's persevering grief — weeping, stooping, looking yet again into the tomb she had found empty — as the very disposition to which the risen Lord draws near. Her inability to leave the place of her loss becomes the occasion of the first resurrection appearance (v.12).

Henry marks the constancy of her affection — Christ appeared first to her (Mk 16:9) — her devotion outlasting the apostles' investigation.

Her inability to leave the place of her loss becomes the very occasion of the first resurrection appearance (v.12).

To her weeping love, and not to the departed apostles, the angels and then the Lord himself will appear (v.12).
12:
"And seeth two angels in white sitting, the one at the head, and the other at the feet, where the body of Jesus had lain." The witnesses read the angelic vision granted to Mary's persevering devotion.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the harmony and the detail. Robertson: the vivid present "beholdeth"; "Peter and John had not seen the two angels," and we are left "to our own ignorance as to the reason why only the women saw them." Ellicott takes it as "a distinct vision to Mary," which she herself related; Matthew and Mark mention one angel, Luke and John two (Matthew 28:5-7; Luke 24:4-8). Lightfoot notes the placing — "one at the head, the other at the feet" — recalls the cherubim at each end of the mercy-seat (Exodus 25:18-19).

Gill adds the Jewish notion that good angels appear "in white apparel," and the angels here in human form. The detail of head and feet, where the body had lain, marks the very place of the resurrection.

The Reformed voice reads the grace. Calvin: "what amazing forbearance displayed by our Lord, in bearing with so many faults in Mary," honouring her by sending his angels and at length making himself known. (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here concerns false Christs and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: the angels, seated like the cherubim that guarded the mercy-seat where atonement was made, mark the empty resting-place of the crucified body as a new holy of holies — and their presence is the first heavenly testimony granted to the weeping woman whom Christ is about to address (v.13).

The witnesses thus see the angels seated like the cherubim that guarded the mercy-seat (Ex 25:18), marking the empty resting-place of the crucified body as a new holy of holies. Their presence is the first heavenly testimony granted to the weeping woman whom Christ is about to address (v.13).

Calvin marvels at Christ's forbearance, honouring her many faults by sending his angels and at length revealing himself to her first of all.
13:
"They say unto her, Woman, why weepest thou? She saith unto them, Because they have taken away my Lord, and I know not where they have laid him." The witnesses read Mary's grief, still fixed on a missing body rather than a risen Lord.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the sense. Robertson notes "I do not know" is singular here (not the plural of v.2), "because clearly Mary is alone." Barnes and Clarke read her assumption that friends had removed the body to another resting-place, perhaps from John 19:42. Ellicott: "the passionate feeling of v.2 still has entire possession of her mind," now even more fervent — "my Lord."

The Reformed voice reads the angels' words. Calvin: the angel's question is "reproof mingled with comfort" — reproving "her excessive" grief, yet tenderly. Gill: they ask "why weepest thou?" since "she had no reason to weep, but to rejoice... for he was risen."

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem sets her among "those noble and brave women" who sought the Lord. So the witnesses agree: Mary's answer reveals a love so absorbed in its loss that it cannot yet conceive of resurrection — she weeps for a stolen body, calling him "my Lord" even in supposed death. The angels' gentle question is the first invitation to look beyond her grief; yet it is not their words but the voice of Christ himself that will turn her sorrow to joy (v.14).

The witnesses thus read Mary's answer as love so absorbed in its loss that it cannot yet conceive of resurrection — she weeps for a stolen body, calling him 'my Lord' even in supposed death. The angels' gentle question is the first invitation to look beyond her grief to the One himself (v.14).

The angels' question is reproof mingled with comfort: she had reason to rejoice, not weep, for the One she mourned was risen (v.14).

Yet it is not the angels' words but the voice of Christ himself that will turn her sorrow into joy (v.16).

Cyril sets her among the noble and brave women who sought the Lord early at the sepulchre.
14:
"And when she had thus said, she turned herself back, and saw Jesus standing, and knew not that it was Jesus." The witnesses read the poignant moment — the risen Lord present, yet unrecognized by his grieving disciple.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scene. Robertson: "she turned herself back" (second aorist passive, "almost reflective"). Barnes: "she was not expecting to see him; it was yet also twilight, and she could not see distinctly" (Luke 24:16). Ellicott: "weighed down by her sorrow, not looking intently," she sees "another form" and turns aside again to weep.

The Reformed voice asks why she did not know him. Calvin: some think "he appeared in a different form, but I think the fault lay rather in the eyes of the women," as with the two on the Emmaus road whose "eyes were holden" (Luke 24:16) — "we will not say that Christ was" transformed, but that her perception was restrained.

So the witnesses agree: the non-recognition is owing not to a change in Christ's body but to Mary's grief, the dim light, and a divine restraining of her eyes — the same that held the Emmaus disciples until the appointed moment. The scene is deeply human: she stands face to face with the One she mourns and does not know him, supposing him the gardener (v.15). The recognition, when it comes, will be by a single word in the familiar voice (v.16), so that faith rests not on sight alone but on the personal call of the Shepherd who knows his own by name.

The witnesses thus trace the non-recognition not to a changed body but to Mary's grief, the dim light, and a divine restraining of her eyes, as with the Emmaus disciples (Lk 24:16). The recognition, when it comes, will be by a single word in the familiar voice (v.16).

The scene is deeply human: she stands face to face with the One she mourns and does not know him, supposing him the gardener (v.15).

The recognition, when it comes, will rest on the personal call of the Shepherd who knows his own by name (John 10:3).
15:
"Jesus saith unto her, Woman, why weepest thou? whom seekest thou? She, supposing him to be the gardener..." The witnesses read the risen Lord drawing out Mary's love before revealing himself.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the touching mistake. Robertson: "Sir" (Kyrie) here "clearly not Lord," for she thought him "the gardener" (kēpouros, keeper of the garden); "a new idea struck Mary as mistaken as the other one" — that he had borne the body away. Ellicott: the "gardener" "corresponded more to what we should call a bailiff," a servant of Joseph of Arimathea. Clarke notes the proverb her words illustrate — "Love feels no load!" — for she would carry the body herself.

The Reformed voice reads her earthbound view. Calvin: she addresses him "according to the custom of her nation," the same word used "in addressing labourers and persons of low condition"; "Mary has no view of this matter but what is earthly."

So the witnesses agree: Christ repeats the angels' question and adds "whom seekest thou?" — drawing forth the depth of Mary's devotion, which would, in its grief, undertake to carry away a corpse single-handed. Her mistake is touching and total: the One she seeks stands before her, and she begs him for directions to the dead. The pathos heightens the joy of the next moment, when a single word — her own name — will pierce the disguise and turn her mourning into recognition (v.16).

The witnesses thus heighten the pathos: the One Mary seeks stands before her, and she begs him for directions to the dead, ready in her love to carry the body herself. The mistake is total and touching, making the joy of the next moment, when her name is spoken, the greater (v.16).

Calvin notes her view of the matter is wholly earthly, addressing the risen Lord as a labourer and begging him for a corpse.

The pathos of the mistake only heightens the joy of the moment when her own name is spoken (v.16).

Christ draws forth the depth of her devotion before piercing her sorrow with a single word (v.16).
16:
"Jesus saith unto her, Mary. She turned herself, and saith unto him, Rabboni; which is to say, Master." The witnesses read the moment of recognition — a single word in the familiar voice, and the Shepherd's call by name.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the words. Robertson: the Aramaic "Mary" (Mariam), "clearly the old familiar tone of Jesus was in the pronunciation of her name"; "Rabboni," Aramaic for "my Teacher." Barnes: "Rabboni" is "my great master," the highest of the Jewish titles of honour. Ellicott: "it is to that devoted love that the first words of the risen Lord are spoken" — he who knew her whole past is near her at the grave-side "while Apostles... were absent."

The Reformed voice reads the method. Calvin: "that Christ allowed Mary a short time to fall into a mistake was useful for confirming her faith; but now, by a single word, he corrects her mistake," assuming "the character of the Master, and addresses his disciple by name." Gill: "he might alter the tone of his voice... calling her by her name in his usual manner" — "Christ has personal knowledge of all his people, and can call them by name."

So the witnesses agree: recognition comes not by sight but by the voice — the Good Shepherd calling his own sheep by name (John 10:3), so that the One she could not know as the gardener she knows instantly as her Master. The single word "Mary," spoken in the remembered tone, accomplishes what argument and angelic vision could not; and her answering "Rabboni" is the cry of a love restored, which Christ must now gently redirect from clinging to commission (v.17).

The witnesses thus make recognition come not by sight but by the voice — the Good Shepherd calling his own by name (John 10:3) — so that a single word, 'Mary,' accomplishes what argument and angelic vision could not. Her answering 'Rabboni' is the cry of a love restored, which Christ must now redirect from clinging to commission (v.17).

The One she could not know as the gardener she knows instantly as her Master, faith resting not on sight alone but on the personal call of the Shepherd.
17:
"Jesus saith unto her, Touch me not; for I am not yet ascended to my Father: but go to my brethren, and say... I ascend unto my Father, and your Father; and to my God, and your God." The witnesses read the much-debated prohibition and the new relationship Christ proclaims.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the verb. Robertson: "cease clinging to me" (present middle imperative), not "do not touch me" — Jesus let the women hold his feet (Matthew 28:9); the prohibition reminds Mary the old fellowship is changed. Clarke: "cling not to me"; Wesley: "do not cling to me... detain me not now; you will have other opportunities." Lightfoot ties it to the promised ascension and sending of the Comforter. Barnes notes the contrast with Thomas (v.27): Mary did not doubt, so needed no touch.

The Eastern and Reformed voices read the new sonship. Cyril of Jerusalem on the distinction: in all things the Son is like the Father, begotten "Life of Life and Light of Light." Calvin reconciles with Matthew — the women were not repelled from Christ "till their eagerness" needed restraint. Gill stresses the careful wording: "my Father, and your Father; my God, and your God" — his by nature, theirs by grace and adoption.

So the witnesses agree: "touch me not" is no denial of his risen body but a redirection — Mary must not cling as if to detain the old earthly fellowship, for he is bound for the Father. And in calling the disciples "my brethren" and naming "my Father and your Father," Christ proclaims the fruit of his resurrection: believers are made the adopted brethren of the Son and children of his God — yet the careful "my" and "your," the fathers insist, keeps the difference between his natural Sonship and their gracious one (v.18).

The witnesses thus read 'touch me not' as redirection, not denial of his body: Mary must not cling to the old fellowship, for he is bound for the Father. And in 'my Father and your Father,' Christ proclaims the resurrection's fruit — believers made his adopted brethren — while the careful 'my' and 'your' guard the difference between his natural Sonship and their gracious one (v.18).
18:
"Mary Magdalene came and told the disciples that she had seen the Lord, and that he had spoken these things unto her." The witnesses read the first commission fulfilled — the weeping woman become the first herald of the resurrection.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the report. Robertson: the present participle "announcing"; "I have seen the Lord" (perfect, heōraka) — "she will always carry in her heart that vision of the Risen Christ," telling the fact "before she delivers Christ's message." Ellicott: "the coming is described from the point of view of the writer, who was one of the disciples."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke notes the reception: Mark records (Mark 16:11) that the disciples "could not believe what she said," treating it "as an effect of her troubled imagination." Gill marks her cheerful obedience — "glad to go on such an errand," though her own love might have desired to stay.

(A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here concerns the feeding of the five thousand and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: Mary, redirected from clinging to commission (v.17), obeys at once and becomes the first witness of the risen Lord — carrying both the fact ("I have seen the Lord") and his message to the brethren. That the apostles at first disbelieved her only underscores the reality of the resurrection: its first heralds were met with incredulity, not credulity, and the proof had to be pressed upon reluctant minds (v.19).

The witnesses thus crown Mary's devotion: redirected from clinging to commission, she obeys at once and becomes the first herald of the risen Lord. That the apostles at first disbelieved her (Mk 16:11) only underscores the reality of the resurrection, whose first witnesses met incredulity, not credulity (v.19).

Robertson notes she will always carry that vision in her heart, telling the fact — 'I have seen the Lord' — before delivering his message to the brethren.

That the apostles disbelieved her at first only underscores that the resurrection's heralds met incredulity, not credulity (Mk 16:11).
19:
"Then the same day at evening... when the doors were shut where the disciples were assembled for fear of the Jews, came Jesus and stood in the midst, and saith, Peace be unto you." The witnesses read the first appearance to the gathered disciples.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the setting. Robertson: the genitive absolute "when it was evening on that day," the resurrection Sunday; John often marks time thus. Barnes: "the doors were shut" — not necessarily that he passed through them, but they were closed for fear, the disciples apprehending the Jews would next turn on them. Ellicott places between this and v.18 the bribing of the guard (Matthew 28:11-15) and the Emmaus road (Luke 24:13-35).

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the comfort. Chrysostom: hearing Mary, they "were obliged either to disbelieve, or, if they believed, to grieve" he had not appeared to them — so he did not delay. Bede: the repeated "Peace" is "a confirmation," Christ being he who "made of twain one."

The Reformed and Puritan voices read the providence and the proof. Calvin: "it did not happen without the providence of God that all were assembled in one place, that the event might be more certain." Henry: "the infallible proof of Christ's resurrection was his showing himself alive" (Acts 1:3). So the witnesses agree: into a room shut fast by fear the risen Lord comes and speaks peace — the very peace he had promised in the upper room (John 14:27; John 16:33) — turning a huddle of frightened disciples into witnesses of his living presence, and grounding the church's gathering on the Lord's Day in his own appearing.

The witnesses thus see the risen Lord enter a room shut fast by fear and speak the very peace he had promised (John 14:27; 16:33), turning frightened disciples into witnesses of his living presence. The first-day appearing grounds the church's gathering on the Lord's Day in his own coming (v.20).

Chrysostom observes he came promptly, lest hearing Mary they should grieve that he had not counted them worthy of the sight (v.20).
20:
"And when he had so said, he shewed unto them his hands and his side. Then were the disciples glad, when they saw the Lord." The witnesses read the proof of bodily identity — and the joy it kindled.

The grammatical-historical readers press the bodily reality. Robertson: "this body, not yet glorified, retained the marks of the nails and the soldier's spear, ample proof of the bodily resurrection against the modern view that only Christ's spirit arose and against the Docetic notion that Jesus had no actual human body." Barnes: "indubitable proofs of his identity" — "the same Being who had suffered." Ellicott notes John alone records the pierced side (John 19:34; v.25).

The Reformed voice reads why the wounds remained. Calvin: if any think it "inconsistent with the glory of Christ" that he bore the marks of his wounds, let him consider "that Christ rose not so much for himself as for us" — the scars are kept as proof and as the abiding memorial of our redemption.

So the witnesses agree: the risen Christ proves his identity by the very wounds of his passion — hands and side — establishing that it is the same body that suffered, truly raised, not a phantom nor a mere spirit. The marks of the cross become the seal of the resurrection, and the disciples' fear gives way to gladness at seeing the Lord. From this joy and proof Christ proceeds to commission them (v.21).

The witnesses thus make the wounds of the passion the seal of the resurrection: hands and side prove it is the same body that suffered, truly raised, no phantom nor mere spirit. Calvin's note holds — the scars are kept because he rose 'not for himself but for us' — and the disciples' fear gives way to gladness (v.21).

Robertson presses the point against Docetism and the 'spirit only' view: this body, not yet glorified, bore the very marks of nails and spear (John 19:34).

The marks of the cross thus become the seal of the resurrection, and the disciples' fear gives way to gladness.
21:
"Then said Jesus to them again, Peace be unto you: as my Father hath sent me, even so send I you." The witnesses read the great apostolic commission — the disciples sent as the Father sent the Son.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the sending. Robertson: Jesus uses both verbs of sending (apostellō, pempō) here "in practically the same sense," still bearing "the Commission of the Father" (perfect). Barnes: "as God sent me to preach, to be persecuted, and to suffer... so I send you" — Christ is "their model." Clarke: "clothed with the very same authority, and influenced by the very same Spirit."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the office and its ground. Calvin: by these words "Christ instals them in the office to which he had appointed them," the repeated "peace" gaining due attention for so weighty a subject. Wesley: "peace in his own soul is the foundation of the mission of a true Gospel minister" (2 Corinthians 4:1); "Christ was the apostle of the Father" (Hebrews 3:1), "Peter and the rest, the apostles of Christ."

So the witnesses agree: the risen Lord sends his disciples into the world on the pattern of his own sending — with the same authority, the same Spirit, the same errand of proclaiming salvation and bearing suffering. The commission is grounded in his own mission from the Father, so that to receive the apostles is to receive Christ, and to receive Christ is to receive the Father who sent him. And lest they attempt so vast a task in their own strength, he at once breathes on them the enabling Spirit (v.22).

The witnesses thus ground the apostolic mission in Christ's own: sent as the Father sent the Son, with the same authority, Spirit, and errand. To receive the apostles is to receive Christ, and Christ the Father; and lest they attempt so vast a task in their own strength, he at once breathes on them the enabling Spirit (v.22).

Calvin: by these words Christ formally instals the apostles in the office to which he had appointed them, the repeated 'peace' gaining due attention.
22:
"And when he had said this, he breathed on them, and saith unto them, Receive ye the Holy Ghost." The witnesses read the symbolic in-breathing — and debate its relation to Pentecost.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the symbol. Robertson: enephysēsen, "a symbolic act with the same word used in the Septuagint when God breathed the breath of life upon Adam" (Genesis 2:7; Ezekiel 37:9). Barnes: "the act of breathing was used to represent the nature of the influence" and "the source of that influence." Clarke: it intimates "they were to be made new men," alluding to the first creation.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads it as foretaste. "As ye receive this breath out of my mouth, so receive ye the Spirit out of my fulness... This was an earnest of Pentecost." The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem: "the fellowship of this Holy Spirit he bestowed on the Apostles."

The Reformed voice reads the equipping. Calvin: "to govern the Church of God, to carry the embassy of eternal salvation... is a task far beyond human capacity," so Christ "prepares the Apostles by the grace of his Spirit." So the witnesses agree: the in-breathing is a real but partial bestowal — an earnest and pledge of the fuller outpouring at Pentecost (Acts 2) — by which the risen Christ, as the second Adam, breathes new life and equips his apostles for the commission just given (v.21). The act echoes creation: as God breathed life into the first man, so Christ breathes the Spirit into the new humanity he is forming.

The witnesses thus read the in-breathing as the act of the second Adam: as God breathed life into the first man (Gen 2:7), so Christ breathes the Spirit into the new humanity he is forming — a real but partial bestowal, the earnest and pledge of the fuller outpouring at Pentecost (Acts 2).

Wesley reads it as 'an earnest of Pentecost' — the Spirit out of Christ's fulness, a pledge of the fuller outpouring to come (Acts 2).

As the first Adam received the breath of life, so the new humanity receives the Spirit from the risen Christ (Gen 2:7).
23:
"Whose soever sins ye remit, they are remitted unto them; and whose soever sins ye retain, they are retained." The witnesses read the "power of the keys" — and here the Catholic/Protestant divide is sharpest.

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read a declarative, gospel-bound authority. Gill: "God only can forgive sins"; the apostles never "assumed any such power," and to do so is "the mark of antichrist," usurping the divine prerogative — their work is to declare the terms of reconciliation. Calvin: this power "must not be separated from the office of teaching"; it is the preaching of the gospel that remits or retains. Wesley: "supposing them to repent and believe, they are remitted; supposing them to remain impenitent, they are retained." Lightfoot ties it to the earlier "binding and loosing."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the grammar and guard against Rome. Robertson: the perfect "are forgiven" (apheōntai) — heaven ratifies what the gospel-minister declares. Barnes: "Jesus confers the same power on all the apostles... if Peter had been appointed to any peculiar authority, it is wonderful that the Saviour did not here hint at any such pre-eminence" (Matthew 16:19; Matthew 18:18).

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem joins it to the gift of the Spirit just bestowed. So the witnesses agree (against a priestly power to absolve at will): the authority is real but ministerial and declarative — exercised through the faithful preaching of the gospel, which announces forgiveness to the penitent believer and retention to the impenitent. It is given to all the apostles alike, with no Petrine primacy, and rests wholly on the Spirit Christ has breathed upon them (v.22).

The witnesses thus read the power of the keys as real but ministerial and declarative — exercised through the faithful preaching of the gospel, which announces forgiveness to the penitent and retention to the impenitent. It is given to all the apostles alike, with no Petrine primacy, and rests wholly on the Spirit just breathed upon them (v.22).

Gill warns that to assume a power to forgive sins outright is 'the mark of antichrist'; the apostles only declare the gospel's terms of reconciliation.
24:
"But Thomas, one of the twelve, called Didymus, was not with them when Jesus came." The witnesses read the absence of Thomas — and the loss it cost him.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the man. Robertson: "Didymus" (twin), "the pessimist of the apostolic band"; "the term twelve is still applied to the group, though Judas is dead." Ellicott: "in harmony with the desponding character that looks upon the visit to Jerusalem as necessarily leading to death" (John 11:16; John 14:5), he is now "not present." Lightfoot notes the name simply means he was called Didymus among the Greeks as Thomas among the Hebrews.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the lesson: "by absenting himself from the company of the disciples, he lost this precious opportunity of seeing and hearing Christ, and of receiving... the inestimable blessing of the Holy Ghost" — "where two or three are assembled in the name of Christ" (Matthew 18:20), he is present.

The Eastern father (Chrysostom) and the Reformed Calvin read his coming unbelief. Calvin: "the unbelief of Thomas is here related, that by means of it the faith of the godly may be more fully confirmed" — he was "not only slow and reluctant... but even obstinate." So the witnesses agree: Thomas's absence from the gathered disciples cost him the joy and the blessing the others received, and his consequent doubt becomes, in God's providence, an occasion for a fuller proof of the resurrection — a warning against forsaking the assembly, and a comfort that Christ stoops to meet even obstinate doubt (v.25).

The witnesses thus make Thomas's absence a costly thing: by forsaking the assembly he lost the joy and blessing the others received (Mt 18:20), and his consequent doubt becomes, in God's providence, the occasion of a fuller proof of the resurrection (v.25).

Robertson calls Thomas 'the pessimist of the apostolic band,' true to the desponding character seen earlier (John 11:16).

His doubt becomes, in God's providence, the occasion of a yet fuller proof of the resurrection (v.27).

The witnesses make it a standing warning against forsaking the assembly of the saints (Heb 10:25).
25:
"...Except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails, and put my finger into the print... I will not believe." The witnesses read Thomas's hardened demand for the evidence of his own senses.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the report. Robertson: the disciples use "the very language Mary had used" (v.18), and Thomas answers with a triple negative condition. Ellicott: his "refusal to admit the testimony of eye-witnesses, though these were the whole of his ten brethren," recalls the demand once made to Christ himself. Barnes reads it as the effect "of deep grief and of that despondency which fills the mind when a long-cherished hope is taken away."

The Reformed voice names the fault. Calvin: "every one wishes to be wise from his own understanding"; Thomas's words "have no approach to faith, but... a sensual judgment" — demanding to see and touch before believing, "altogether at variance with the nature of faith." Lightfoot recalls the rabbinic saying that even Moses was charged, "if I do not see, I will not believe."

So the witnesses agree: Thomas's demand is no mere caution but an obstinate refusal of sufficient testimony — the word of ten trustworthy brethren who had seen the Lord. His insistence on sight and touch sets the standard of unbelief against which Christ will pronounce the blessing on those who believe without seeing (v.29); yet his very stubbornness, the witnesses note, occasions a fresh and gracious condescension of the risen Lord (v.26).

The witnesses thus read Thomas's demand as no mere caution but an obstinate refusal of sufficient testimony — the word of ten trustworthy brethren. His insistence on sight and touch sets the standard of unbelief against which Christ will pronounce the blessing on those who believe without seeing (v.29).

Calvin names it a 'sensual judgment' wholly at variance with faith — demanding to see and touch before he will believe the word of ten brethren.

His demand sets the standard of unbelief against which the blessing on those who have not seen will shine (v.29).
26:
"And after eight days again his disciples were within, and Thomas with them: then came Jesus, the doors being shut, and stood in the midst, and said, Peace be unto you." The witnesses read the second appearance — and the weekly gathering it implies.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the time. Robertson: "after eight days" is "the next Sunday evening," reckoned inclusively like "after three days"; the scene "precisely as in v.19 save Thomas was with them." Barnes and Clarke see here the pattern of the weekly meeting on the first day, Jesus countenancing it by appearing twice; Ellicott: "the octave of the first appearance... the first Sunday after Easter." Lightfoot doubts the "doors shut" means his body penetrated them, taking it rather as the disciples' continued fear.

The Eastern father reads the condescension. Chrysostom: "because one person, Thomas, would examine him, Christ allowed him," exhibiting his wounds to "one soul."

The Reformed and Puritan voices read the grace. Calvin: "when Christ so readily yields to the improper request of Thomas... we learn how earnestly" he seeks to overcome unbelief. Henry marks "another appearance of Christ to his disciples, when Thomas was now with them." So the witnesses agree: a week later, the disciples again assembled — now with Thomas — and the risen Lord comes once more with "Peace," graciously meeting the doubter on the very terms he had set. The repeated first-day appearance quietly commends the Lord's-Day assembly, and the patient condescension of Christ shames and heals Thomas's unbelief (v.27).

The witnesses thus see, a week later, the disciples again assembled — now with Thomas — and the risen Lord coming once more with 'Peace,' graciously meeting the doubter on his own terms. The repeated first-day appearance commends the Lord's-Day assembly, and Christ's patient condescension shames and heals unbelief (v.27).

Calvin reads in Christ's ready yielding to the improper request how earnestly he labours to overcome unbelief (v.27).

The repeated first-day appearance quietly commends the Lord's-Day assembly of the disciples (v.19).

Christ's patient condescension to the doubter both shames and heals his unbelief (v.28).
27:
"Then saith he to Thomas, Reach hither thy finger... and be not faithless, but believing." The witnesses read Christ's gracious yet searching word to the doubter — meeting his exact terms and calling him to faith.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the force. Robertson: Jesus "turns directly to Thomas as if he had come expressly for his sake," revealing "his knowledge of the doubt" and naming "the very tests" Thomas had set (v.25); "be not faithless" (present middle, "stop becoming disbelieving"). Ellicott: this knowledge of Thomas's words "in itself would carry conviction," and "a sense of shame at his unbelief."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the compassion: "through his infinite compassion, he addressed him in a particular manner, condescending... to accommodate himself to the prejudices of an obstinate, though sincere, disciple," and "it is very probable that Thomas did" reach out, "for his unbelief was too deeply rooted to be easily cured."

Gill notes Christ called Thomas "by name in the most friendly manner, without upbraiding" him. So the witnesses agree: the risen Lord meets the doubter on his own terms — offering the very hands and side, the nail-prints and the spear-wound, that Thomas had demanded — yet not without the loving rebuke, "be not faithless, but believing." The condescension is complete, but its aim is faith: Christ stoops to the evidence of the senses only to lift Thomas to the confession that surpasses it (v.28).

The witnesses thus read the condescension as complete but aimed at faith: Christ offers the very nail-prints and spear-wound Thomas demanded, yet with the loving rebuke 'be not faithless, but believing.' He stoops to the evidence of the senses only to lift Thomas to the confession that surpasses it (v.28).

Gill notes he called Thomas by name in the friendliest manner, without upbraiding him for his unbelief, that he might win and not crush him.

He stoops to the evidence of the senses only to lift the doubter to the confession that surpasses it (v.28).

The aim of the condescension is not to satisfy curiosity but to produce faith in a sincere though obstinate disciple.
28:
"And Thomas answered and said unto him, My Lord and my God." The witnesses read the climactic confession — the fullest acknowledgment of Christ's deity in the Gospel.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the address. Robertson: "not exclamation, but address, the vocative... Thomas was wholly convinced and did not hesitate to address the Risen Christ as Lord and God. And Jesus accepts the words and praises Thomas." Barnes lists the reasons it proves Christ's divinity: it was no mere expression of surprise, but spoken "to him," and Jesus did not rebuke it. Ellicott: "it is implied that he did not make use of the tests... but at once expressed the fulness of his conviction."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke refutes the Socinian gloss: "those who deny the Godhead of Christ would have us believe these words are an exclamation... addressed to the Father" — but Theodore of Mopsuestia, who first so taught, was condemned. Wesley: Thomas "confesses his Godhead, and that more explicitly than any other had yet done... without putting his hand" to the test.

So the witnesses agree: Thomas, overcome, makes the great confession — "My Lord and my God" — addressed directly to the risen Christ, who receives it as due worship. The doubter becomes the boldest confessor of Christ's deity, and his words, spoken without recourse to the offered proof, stand as the Gospel's climactic testimony to who Jesus is. That Christ accepts rather than corrects the title settles the matter: he is Lord and God (v.29).

The witnesses thus make Thomas the boldest confessor: overcome, he addresses the risen Christ directly as 'My Lord and my God,' and Christ receives it as due worship rather than correcting it. That acceptance settles the matter — Jesus is Lord and God — and his words, spoken without recourse to the offered proof, stand as the Gospel's climactic testimony (v.29).

Clarke refutes the Socinian gloss that the words were a mere exclamation addressed to the Father — a reading condemned in Theodore of Mopsuestia.

That Christ accepts rather than corrects the title settles the matter: he is truly Lord and God.
29:
"Jesus saith unto him... because thou hast seen me, thou hast believed: blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed." The witnesses read Christ's commendation of a faith that rests on testimony rather than sight.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the words. Robertson: probably interrogative — "because thou hast seen me, hast thou believed?" — and "it was sight, not touch, that convinced Thomas"; he "missed the highest form of faith," while Peter commends believing without seeing (1 Peter 1:8). Barnes: Jesus "approves the faith of Thomas, but more highly commends the faith of those who should believe without having seen"; "blessed" has "the force of" a benediction. Ellicott reads it as a gentle question.

The Reformed voice guards the nature of faith. Calvin: Christ "blames nothing in Thomas but that he was so slow to believe, that he needed to be violently drawn... by the experience of the senses," which is "at variance with the nature of faith." Lightfoot adds the rabbinic esteem of the proselyte who believed without the thunders of Sinai.

So the witnesses agree: Christ accepts Thomas's faith but pronounces a higher blessing on all who, in every age, believe the apostolic testimony without the evidence of sight — the very faith this Gospel was written to produce (v.31). The beatitude looks beyond the upper room to the whole church, founding its faith not on seeing the risen body but on the trustworthy witness of those who did. Sight convinced Thomas; the word will convince the world.

The witnesses thus hear the beatitude reach beyond the upper room to the whole church: a higher blessing on all who believe the apostolic testimony without the evidence of sight (1 Pet 1:8). Sight convinced Thomas; the word will convince the world — the very faith this Gospel was written to produce (v.31).

Calvin: Christ blames Thomas's slowness, that he needed to be 'violently drawn to faith by the experience of the senses,' which faith's nature forbids.

The beatitude looks past the upper room to the whole church, whose faith rests on the trustworthy witness of those who saw.
30:
"And many other signs truly did Jesus in the presence of his disciples, which are not written in this book." The witnesses read the Evangelist's acknowledgment that his record is selective, not exhaustive.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scope. Robertson: "many other signs" beyond those described — alluded to in John 2:23, John 4:45, John 12:37 — "are not written in this book." Ellicott: the signs are "not the proofs of the Resurrection only, but the works wrought during the whole life"; "the writer's narrative is drawing to a close." Barnes: many were recorded by the other evangelists, "and many... were never recorded" (John 21:25).

The Latin father and the Puritan read the purpose of selection. Augustine of Hippo notes that after the incident with Thomas the Evangelist begins to sum up. Henry: "these were committed to writing, that all might believe."

The Reformed voice reads the honesty. Calvin: but for this note, readers "might have supposed that he had left out none of the miracles" and "had given a full and complete account"; John "testifies that he has only related some things out of a large number." So the witnesses agree: the Gospel is a deliberate, Spirit-guided selection, not an exhaustive chronicle — John chose from a great store of signs those that would best serve his aim. The acknowledgment both explains the narrative's apparent abruptness and underscores its purpose, which the next verse states outright (v.31).

The witnesses thus read the verse as the Evangelist's candid acknowledgment that his record is a deliberate, Spirit-guided selection, not an exhaustive chronicle (John 21:25). He chose from a great store of signs those that would best serve his aim, which the next verse states outright (v.31).

Barnes notes many signs were recorded by the other evangelists, and many more never written at all (John 21:25).

The acknowledgment both explains the narrative's apparent abruptness and underscores its deliberate purpose (v.31).

The Gospel is thus an instrument of faith, not a complete chronicle, every chosen sign serving its saving end (v.31).
31:
"But these are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name." The witnesses read the Gospel's own statement of its purpose — its thesis and end.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the aim. Barnes: "a clue to the design John had in view" — to prove "that Jesus was the Messiah" and that believers "might be convinced and have eternal life." Robertson: the present subjunctive, "that you may keep on believing"; "the book has had precisely this effect of continuous confirmation of faith." Ellicott: "the writer's own statement of his object," explaining "what seems an abrupt end."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke: it is written "to give full proof of the Divinity of Christ; that he is the promised Messiah... and that through him every believer might have eternal life" — many copies read "eternal life." The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem fixes the confession against the Jews who allow Jesus but not the Christ.

The Reformed voice reads the sufficiency. Calvin: John "committed to writing what ought to satisfy us, because it is abundantly sufficient for confirming our faith," replying "to the vain curiosity" that craves more. So the witnesses agree: the whole Gospel was written with a twofold, saving aim — that its readers might believe Jesus to be the Christ, the Son of God, and so, believing, have life in his name. The signs selected (v.30) all serve this end; the book is not a biography but an instrument of faith and life, and stands complete for that purpose, the climax of the confession of Thomas (v.28) made the church's own.

The witnesses thus read the Gospel's stated purpose as twofold and saving: that its readers might believe Jesus to be the Christ, the Son of God, and so, believing, have life in his name. The book is not a biography but an instrument of faith and life, and stands complete for that end — Thomas's confession (v.28) made the church's own.

Calvin reads the sufficiency: John wrote what is 'abundantly sufficient for confirming our faith,' answering the vain curiosity that always craves more.
